## - MULTIVARIATE PROBABILISTIC TIME SERIES FORE CASTING VIA CONDITIONED NORMALIZING FLOWS

## **Kashif Rasul, Abdul-Saboor Sheikh, Ingmar Schuster, Urs Bergmann & Roland Vollgraf**

Zalando Research M¨uhlenstraße 25 10243 Berlin Germany _{_ kashif.rasul, ingmar.schuster, roland.vollgraf _}_ @zalando.de

## ABSTRACT

Time series forecasting is often fundamental to scientific and engineering problems and enables decision making. With ever increasing data set sizes, a trivial solution to scale up predictions is to assume independence between interacting time series. However, modeling statistical dependencies can improve accuracy and enable analysis of interaction effects. Deep learning methods are well suited for this problem, but multivariate models often assume a simple parametric distribution and do not scale to high dimensions. In this work we model the multivariate temporal dynamics of time series via an autoregressive deep learning model, where the data distribution is represented by a conditioned normalizing flow. This combination retains the power of autoregressive models, such as good performance in extrapolation into the future, with the flexibility of flows as a general purpose high-dimensional distribution model, while remaining computationally tractable. We show that it improves over the state-of-the-art for standard metrics on many real-world data sets with several thousand interacting time-series.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Classical time series forecasting methods such as those in Hyndman & Athanasopoulos (2018) typically provide univariate forecasts and require hand-tuned features to model seasonality and other parameters. Time series models based on recurrent neural networks (RNN), like LSTM (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber, 1997), have become popular methods due to their end-to-end training, the ease of incorporating exogenous covariates, and their automatic feature extraction abilities, which are the hallmarks of deep learning. Forecasting outputs can either be points or probability distributions, in which case the forecasts typically come with uncertainty bounds.

The problem of modeling uncertainties in time series forecasting is of vital importance for assessing how much to trust the predictions for downstream tasks, such as anomaly detection or (business) decision making. Without probabilistic modeling, the importance of the forecast in regions of low noise (small variance around a mean value) versus a scenario with high noise cannot be distinguished. Hence, point estimation models ignore risk stemming from this noise, which would be of particular importance in some contexts such as making (business) decisions.

Finally, individual time series, in many cases, are statistically dependent on each other, and models need the capacity to adapt to this in order to improve forecast accuracy (Tsay, 2014). For example, to model the demand for a retail article, it is important to not only model its sales dependent on its own past sales, but also to take into account the effect of interacting articles, which can lead to _cannibalization_ effects in the case of article competition. As another example, consider traffic flow in a network of streets as measured by occupancy sensors. A disruption on one particular street will also ripple to occupancy sensors of nearby streets—a univariate model would arguably not be able to account for these effects.

In this work, we propose end-to-end trainable autoregressive deep learning architectures for _probabilistic forecasting_ that explicitly models multivariate time series and their temporal dynamics by employing a normalizing flow, like the Masked Autoregressive Flow (Papamakarios et al., 2017) or

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Real NVP (Dinh et al., 2017). These models are able to scale to thousands of interacting time series, we show that they are able to learn ground-truth dependency structure on toy data and we establish new state-of-the-art results on diverse real world data sets by comparing to competitive baselines. Additionally, these methods adapt to a broad class of underlying data distribution on account of using a normalizing flow and our Transformer based model is highly efficient due to the parallel nature of attention layers while training.

The paper first provides some background context in Section 2. We cover related work in Section 3. Section 4 introduces our model and the experiments are detailed in Section 5. We conclude with some discussion in Section 6. The Appendix contains details of the datasets, additional metrics and exploratory plots of forecast intervals as well as details of our model.

## 2 BACKGROUND

## 2.1 DENSITY ESTIMATION VIA NORMALIZING FLOWS

Normalizing flows (Tabak & Turner, 2013; Papamakarios et al., 2019) are mappings from R _[D]_ to R _[D]_ such that densities _pX_ on the input space _X_ = R _[D]_ are transformed into some simple distribution _pZ_ (e.g. an isotropic Gaussian) on the space _Z_ = R _[D]_ . These mappings, _f_ : _X �→Z_ , are composed of a sequence of bijections or invertible functions. Due to the change of variables formula we can express _pX_ ( **x** ) by

where _∂f_ ( **x** ) _/∂_ **x** is the Jacobian of _f_ at **x** . Normalizing flows have the property that the inverse **x** = _f[−]_[1] ( **z** ) is easy to evaluate and computing the Jacobian determinant takes _O_ ( _D_ ) time.

The bijection introduced by Real NVP (Dinh et al., 2017) called the _coupling layer_ satisfies the above two properties. It leaves part of its inputs unchanged and transforms the other part via functions of the un-transformed variables (with superscript denoting the coordinate indices)

where _⊙_ is an element wise product, _s_ () is a scaling and _t_ () a translation function from R _[d] �→_ R _[D][−][d]_ , given by neural networks. To model a nonlinear density map _f_ ( **x** ), a number of coupling layers which map _X �→Y_ 1 _�→· · · �→YK−_ 1 _�→Z_ are composed together all the while alternating the dimensions which are unchanged and transformed. This model, parameterized by the weights of the scaling and translation neural networks _θ_ , is then trained via stochastic gradient descent (SGD) on training data points where for each batch _D_ we maximize the average log likelihood (1) given by

In practice, Batch Normalization (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015) is applied as a bijection to outputs of successive coupling layers to stabilize the training of normalizing flows. This bijection implements the normalization procedure using a weighted moving average of the layer’s mean and standard deviation values, which has to be adapted to either training or inference regimes.

2

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

The Real NVP approach can be generalized, resulting in Masked Autoregressive Flows (Papamakarios et al., 2017) (MAF) where the transformation layer is built as an autoregressive neural network in the sense that it takes in some input **x** _∈_ R _[D]_ and outputs **y** = ( _y_[1] _, . . . , y[D]_ ) with the requirement that this transformation is invertible and any output _y[i] cannot_ depend on input with dimension indices _≥ i_ , i.e. **x** _[≥][i]_ . The Jacobian of this transformation is triangular and thus the Jacobian determinant is tractable. Instead of using a RNN to share parameters across the _D_ dimensions of **x** one avoids this sequential computation by using masking, giving the method its name. The inverse however, needed for generating samples, is sequential.

By realizing that the scaling and translation function approximators don’t need to be invertible, it is straight-forward to implement conditioning of the PDF _pX_ ( **x** _|_ **h** ) on some additional information **h** _∈_ R _[H]_ : we concatenate **h** to the inputs of the scaling and translation function approximators of the coupling layers, i.e. _s_ ( `concat` ( **x**[1:] _[d] ,_ **h** )) and _t_ ( `concat` ( **x**[1:] _[d] ,_ **h** )) which are modified to map R _[d]_[+] _[H] �→_ R _[D][−][d]_ . Another approach is to add a bias computed from **h** to every layer inside the _s_ and _t_ networks as proposed by Korshunova et al. (2018). This does not change the log-determinant of the coupling layers given by (2). More importantly for us, for sequential data, indexed by _t_ , we can share parameters across the different conditioners **h** _t_ by using RNNs or Attention in an autoregressive fashion.

For discrete data the distribution has differential entropy of negative infinity, which leads to arbitrary high likelihood when training normalizing flow models, even on test data. To avoid this one can _dequantize_ the data, often by adding Uniform[0 _,_ 1) noise to integer-valued data. The log-likelihood of the resulting continuous model is then lower-bounded by the log-likelihood of the discrete one as shown in Theis et al. (2016).

## 2.2 SELF-ATTENTION

The self-attention based Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) model has been used for sequence modeling with great success. The multi-head self-attention mechanism enables it to capture both longand short-term dependencies in time series data. Essentially, the Transformer takes in a sequence **X** = [ **x** 1 _, . . . ,_ **x** _T_ ][⊺] _∈_ R _[T][ ×][D]_ , and the multi-head self-attention transforms this into _H_ distinct query **Q** _h_ = **XW** _h[Q]_[, key] **[ K]** _[h]_[=] **[ XW]** _h[K]_[and value] **[ V]** _[h]_[=] **[ XW]** _h[V]_[matrices, where the] **[ W]** _h[Q]_[,] **[ W]** _h[K]_[, and] **[ W]** _h[V]_ are learnable parameters. After these linear projections the scaled dot-product attention computes a sequence of vector outputs via:

where a mask **M** can be applied to filter out right-ward attention (or future information leakage) by setting its upper-triangular elements to _−∞_ and we normalize by _dK_ the dimension of the **W** _h[K]_ matrices. Afterwards, all _H_ outputs **O** _h_ are concatenated and linearly projected again.

One typically uses the Transformer in an encoder-decoder setup, where some warm-up time series is passed through the encoder and the decoder can be used to learn and autoregressively generate outputs.

## 3 RELATED WORK

Related to this work are models that combine normalizing flows with sequential modeling in some way. Transformation Autoregressive Networks (Oliva et al., 2018) which model the density of a multi-variate variable **x** _∈_ R _[D]_ as _D_ conditional distributions Π _[D] i_ =1 _[p][X]_[ (] _[x][i][|][x][i][−]_[1] _[, . . . , x]_[1][)][, where the] conditioning is given by a mixture model coming from the state of a RNN, and is then transformed via a bijection. The PixelSNAIL (Chen et al., 2018) method also models the joint as a product of conditional distributions, optionally with some global conditioning, via causal convolutions and self-attention (Vaswani et al., 2017) to capture long-term temporal dependencies. These methods are well suited to modeling high dimensional data like images, however their use in modeling the temporal development of data has only recently been explored for example in VideoFlow (Kumar et al., 2019) in which they model the distribution of the next video frame via a flow where the model outputs the parameters of the flow’s base distribution via a ConvNet, whereas our approach will be based on conditioning of the PDF as described above.

3

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Using RNNs for modeling either multivariate or temporal dynamics introduces sequential computational dependencies that are not amenable to parallelization. Despite this, RNNs have been shown to be very effective in modeling sequential dynamics. A recent work in this direction (Hwang et al., 2019) employs bipartite flows with RNNs for temporal conditioning to develop a conditional generative model of multivariate sequential data. The authors use a bidirectional training procedure to learn a generative model of observations that together with the temporal conditioning through a RNN, can also be conditioned on (observed) covariates that are modeled as additional conditioning variables in the latent space, which adds extra padding dimensions to the normalizing flow.

The other aspect of related works deals with multivariate probabilistic time series methods which are able to model high dimensional data. The Gaussian Copula Process method (Salinas et al., 2019a) is a RNN-based time series method with a Gaussian copula process output modeled using a low-rank covariance structure to reduce computational complexity and handle non-Gaussian marginal distributions. By using a low-rank approximation of the covariance matrix they obtain a computationally tractable method and are able to scale to multivariate dimensions in the thousands with state-of-the-art results. We will compare our model to this method in what follows.

## 4 TEMPORAL CONDITIONED NORMALIZING FLOWS

We denote the entities of a multivariate time series by _x[i] t[∈]_[R][ for] _[ i][ ∈{]_[1] _[, . . . , D][}]_[ where] _[ t]_[ is the time] index. Thus the multivariate vector at time _t_ is given by **x** _t ∈_ R _[D]_ . We will in what follows consider time series with _t ∈_ [1 _, T_ ], sampled from the complete time series history of our data, where for training we will split this time series by some context window [1 _, t_ 0) and prediction window [ _t_ 0 _, T_ ].

In the DeepAR model (Salinas et al., 2019b), the log-likelihood of each entity _x[i] t_[at][a][time][step] _t ∈_ [ _t_ 0 _, T_ ] is maximized given an individual time series’ prediction window. This is done with respect to the parameters of the chosen distributional model (e.g. negative binomal for count data) via the state of a RNN derived from its previous time step _x[i] t−_ 1[and current covariates] **[ c]** _[i] t_[.][The emission] distribution model, which is typically Gaussian for real-valued data or negative binomial for count data, is selected to best match the statistics of the time series and the network incorporates activation functions that satisfy the constraints of these distribution parameters, e.g. a softplus() for the scale parameter of the Gaussian.

A simple model for multivariate real-valued data could use a factorizing distribution in the emissions. Shared parameters can then learn patterns across the individual time series through the temporal component—but the model falls short of capturing dependencies in the emissions of the model. For this, a full joint distribution at each time step must be modeled, for example by using a multivariate Gaussian model. However, modeling the full covariance matrix not only increases the number of parameters of the neural network by _O_ ( _D_[2] ), making learning difficult, but computing the loss becomes expensive when _D_ is large. Furthermore, statistical dependencies in the emissions would be limited to second-order effects. These models are referred to as Vec-LSTM in Salinas et al. (2019a).

We wish to have a scalable model of _D_ interacting time-series **x** _t_ , and further to use a flexible distribution model on the emissions that allows for capturing and representing higher order moments. To this end, we model the conditional joint distribution at time _t_ of all time series _pX_ ( **x** _t|_ **h** _t_ ; _θ_ ) with a flow, e.g. a Real NVP, conditioned on either the hidden state of a RNN at time _t_ or an embedding of the time series up to _t −_ 1 from an attention module. In the case of an autoregressive RNN (either a LSTM or a GRU (Chung et al., 2014)), its hidden state **h** _t_ is updated given the previous time step observation **x** _t−_ 1 and the current time step’s covariates **c** _t_ (as in Figure 1):

This model is autoregressive since it consumes the observation of the last time step **x** _t−_ 1 as well as the recurrent state **h** _t−_ 1 to produce the state **h** _t_ on which we condition the current observation.

To get a powerful and general emission distribution model, we stack _K_ layers of a conditional flow module (Real NVP or MAF) and together with the RNN, we arrive at our model of the conditional distribution of the future of all time series, given its past _t ∈_ [1 _, t_ 0) and all the covariates in _t ∈_ [1 _, T_ ]. As the model is autoregressive it can be written as a product of factors

where _θ_ denotes the set of all parameters of both the flow and the RNN.

4

For modeling the time evolution, we also investigate an encoder-decoder Transformer (Vaswani et al., 2017) architecture where the encoder embeds **x** 1: _t_ 0 _−_ 1 and the decoder outputs the conditioning for the flow over **x** _t_ 0: _T_ via a masked attention module. See Figure 2 for a schematic of the overall model in this case. While training, care has to be taken to prevent using information from future time points as well as to preserve the autoregressive property by utilizing a mask that reflects the causal direction of the progressing time, i.e. to mask out future time points. The Transformer allows the model to access any part of the historic time series regardless of temporal distance (Li et al., 2019) and thus is potentially able to generate better conditioning for the normalizing flow head.

The Transformer has computational complexity _O_ ( _T_[2] _D_ ) compared to a RNN which is _O_ ( _TD_[2] ), where _T_ is the time series length and the assumption that the dimension of the hidden states are proportional to the number of simultaneous time-series modeled. This means for large multivariate time series, i.e. _D > T_ , the Transformer flow model has smaller computational complexity and unlike the RNN, all computation while training, over the time dimension happens in _parallel_ .

## 4.2 COVARIATES

We employ embeddings for categorical features (Charrington, 2018), which allows for relationships within a category, or its context, to be captured while training models. Combining these embeddings

as features for time series forecasting yields powerful models like the first place winner of the Kaggle Taxi Trajectory Prediction[1] challenge (De Brebisson´ et al., 2015). The covariates **c** _t_ we use are composed of time-dependent (e.g. day of week, hour of day) and time-independent embeddings, if applicable, as well as lag features depending on the time frequency of the data set we are training on. All covariates are thus known for the time periods we wish to forecast.

## 4.3 INFERENCE

For inference we either obtain the hidden state **h**[ˆ] _t_ 1 by passing a “warm up” time series **x** 1: _t_ 1 _−_ 1 through the RNN or use the cold-start hidden state, i.e. we set **h**[ˆ] _t_ 1 = **h** 1 = _[⃗]_ 0, and then by sampling a noise vector **z** _t_ 1 _∈_ R _[D]_ from an isotropic Gaussian, go backward through the flow to obtain a sample ˆ of our time series for the next time step, **x** _t_ 1 = _f[−]_[1] ( **z** _t_ 1 _|_ **h**[ˆ] _t_ 1), conditioned on this starting state. We then use this sample and its covariates to obtain the next conditioning state **h**[ˆ] _t_ 1+1 via the RNN and repeat till our inference horizon. This process of sampling trajectories from some initial state can be repeated many times to obtain empirical quantiles of the uncertainty of our prediction for arbitrary long forecast horizons.

The attention model similarly uses a warm-up time series **x** 1: _t_ 1 _−_ 1 and covariates and passes them through the encoder and then uses the decoder to output the conditioning for sampling from the flow. This sample is then used again in the decoder to iteratively sample the next conditioning state, similar to the inference procedure in seq-to-seq models.

Note that we do _not_ sample from a reduced-temperature model, e.g. by scaling the variance of the isotropic Gaussian, unlike what is done in likelihood-based generative models (Parmar et al., 2018) to obtain higher quality samples.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

Here we discuss a toy experiment for sanity-checking our model and evaluate probabilistic forecasting results on six real-world data sets with competitive baselines. The source code of the model, as well as other time series models, is available at https://github.com/zalandoresearch/ pytorch-ts.

We compare our method using LSTM and two different normalizing flows (LSTM-Real-NVP and LSTM-MAF based on Real NVP and MAF, respectively) as well as a Transformer model with MAF (Transformer-MAF), with the most _competitive_ baseline probabilistic models from Salinas et al. (2019a) on the six data sets and report the results in Table 1. Vec-LSTM-ind-scaling outputs the parameters of an _independent_ Gaussian distribution with mean-scaling, Vec-LSTM-lowrank-Copula parametrizes a low-rank plus diagonal covariance via Copula process. GP-scaling unrolls a LSTM with scaling on each individual time series before reconstructing the joint distribution via a low-rank Gaussian. Similarly, GP-Copula unrolls a LSTM on each individual time series and then the joint emission distribution is given by a low-rank plus diagonal covariance Gaussian copula.

Figure 5: Visual analysis of the dependency structure extrapolation of the model. _Left_ : Crosscovariance matrix computed from the test split of Traffic benchmark. _Middle_ : Cross-covariance matrix computed from the mean of 100 sample trajectories drawn from the Transformer-MAF model’s extrapolation into the future (test split). _Right_ : The absolute difference of the two matrices mostly shows small deviations between ground-truth and extrapolation.

In Table 1 we observe that MAF with either RNN or self-attention mechanism for temporal conditioning achieves the state-of-the-art (to the best of our knowledge) CRPSsum on all benchmarks. Moreover, bipartite flows with RNN either also outperform or are found to be competitive w.r.t. the previous state-of-the-art results as listed in the first four columns of Table 1. Further analyses with other metrics (e.g. CRPS and MSE) are reported in Section B of the appendix.

To showcase how well our model captures dependencies in extrapolating the time series into the future versus real data, we plot in Figure 5 the cross-covariance matrix of observations (plotted left) as well as the mean of 100 sample trajectories (middle plot) drawn from Transformer-MAF model for the test split of Traffic data set. As can be seen, most of the covariance structure especially in the top-left region of highly correlated sensors is very well reflected in the samples drawn from the model.

## 6 CONCLUSION

We have presented a general method to model high-dimensional probabilistic multivariate time series by combining conditional normalizing flows with an autoregressive model, such as a recurrent neural network or an attention module. Autoregressive models have a long-standing reputation for working very well for time series forecasting, as they show good performance in extrapolation into the future. The flow model, on the other hand, does not assume any simple fixed distribution class, but instead can adapt to a broad range of high-dimensional data distributions. The combination hence combines the extrapolation power of the autoregressive model class with the density estimation flexibility of flows. Furthermore, it is computationally efficient, without the need of resorting to approximations (e.g. low-rank approximations of a covariance structure as in Gaussian copula methods) and is robust compared to Deep Kernel learning methods especially for large _D_ . Analysis on six commonly used time series benchmarks establishes the new state-of-the-art performance against competitive methods.
A natural way to improve our method is to incorporate a better underlying flow model. For example, Table 1 shows that swapping the Real NVP flow with a MAF improved the performance, which is a

consequence of Real NVP lacking in density modeling performance compared to MAF. Likewise, we would expect other design choices of the flow model to improve performance, e.g. changes to the dequantization method, the specific affine coupling layer or more expressive conditioning, say via another Transformer. Recent improvements to flows, e.g. as proposed in the Flow++ (Ho et al., 2019), to obtain expressive bipartite flow models, or models to handle discrete categorical data (Tran et al., 2019), are left as future work to assess their usefulness. To our knowledge, it is however still an open problem how to model discrete ordinal data via flows—which would best capture the nature of some data sets (e.g. sales data).

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: This paper presents a timely and technically sound contribution to the field of multivariate probabilistic time series forecasting by combining autoregressive deep learning architectures with conditioned normalizing flows—a powerful synergy that addresses key limitations in scalability, flexibility, and dependency modeling. The clarity of exposition is strong overall: the motivation is well-grounded in real-world applications such as retail demand and traffic flow, and the technical background on normalizing flows is concise yet sufficient for readers familiar with deep generative models. The proposed method effectively leverages the expressiveness of normalizing flows—specifically Real NVP and MAF—to model complex, high-dimensional distributions while maintaining computational tractability through conditioning on temporal dynamics via an autoregressive framework. A major strength lies in the model’s ability to capture inter-series dependencies without sacrificing scalability, demonstrated convincingly across datasets with thousands of time series. However, a significant limitation not fully addressed is the potential inefficiency in autoregressive sampling due to the sequential nature of generating from MAFs, which could hinder real-time forecasting applications despite the use of parallelizable components like Transformers during training. Additionally, while the paper claims state-of-the-art performance, more ablation studies would strengthen the analysis—particularly regarding the relative contributions of the flow architecture versus the conditioning mechanism. The treatment of exogenous covariates and non-stationarity, though mentioned, remains underdeveloped, leaving open questions about robustness in dynamic environments. Overall, the paper is well-structured and makes a clear advance over existing approaches, but would benefit from deeper discussion on inference speed, model complexity, and generalization across domain-specific forecasting challenges.

comments.title: Misleading claim about general normalizing flow Jacobian complexity
comments.quote: Normalizing flows have the property that the inverse **x** = _f[−]_[1] ( **z** ) is easy to evaluate and computing the Jacobian determinant takes _O_ ( _D_ ) time.
comments.explanation: The statement claims that Jacobian determinant computation takes O(D) time for general normalizing flows, but this efficiency is not universal — it only holds for specific architectures like Real NVP or MAF due to their structured (triangular or block-triangular) Jacobians. For arbitrary bijections, determinant computation is O(D^3). While the next paragraph narrows focus to Real NVP (where O(D) applies), the initial broad statement could mislead readers into believing computational tractability is a universal feature of all flows. This imprecision risks perpetuating a common misconception, especially among less experienced readers, despite partial contextual correction.

comments.title: Incorrect direction of dequantization log-likelihood bound
comments.quote: The log-likelihood of the resulting continuous model is then lower-bounded by the log-likelihood of the discrete one as shown in Theis et al. (2016)
comments.explanation: The paper incorrectly claims that the dequantized continuous model's log-likelihood is *lower-bounded* by the discrete model's, when the correct result (from Theis et al., 2016) is that it is an *upper bound* (up to a constant). Specifically, log p_Y(y) ≥ log p_X(x) + const, meaning maximizing the continuous likelihood optimizes an upper bound on the discrete entropy. Stating it as a lower bound inverts the statistical relationship and undermines the theoretical justification for dequantization. While this may be a verbal slip, it misrepresents the variational nature of the approach and could mislead readers about the validity and direction of the bound.

comments.title: Incomplete RNN state update description omits recurrent dependence
comments.quote: its hidden state **h** _t_ is updated given the previous time step observation **x** _t−_ 1 and the current time step’s covariates **c** _t_
comments.explanation: The RNN update is described as depending only on **x**_{t-1} and **c_t}, but omits the essential dependence on the previous hidden state **h**_{t−1}, which is fundamental to recurrence. While Figure 1 and a later sentence mention the use of **h**_{t−1}, the textual description of the update function fails to include it, suggesting a memoryless update. This omission could mislead readers into thinking the model is not truly recurrent or that **h_t** is computed without carryover from earlier states. Although the recurrence is acknowledged elsewhere, the absence of **h**_{t−1} in the update expression constitutes a significant underspecification that affects model reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Transformer inference procedure undermines claimed parallelism
comments.quote: The attention model similarly uses a warm-up time series **x** 1: _t_ 1 _−_ 1 and covariates and passes them through the encoder and then uses the decoder to output the conditioning for sampling from the flow. This sample is then used again in the decoder to iteratively sample the next conditioning state, similar to the inference procedure in seq-to-seq models.
comments.explanation: The description suggests an autoregressive decoding loop where sampled outputs are fed back into the decoder, which contradicts the paper’s emphasis on parallel inference with Transformers. If inference requires iterative feeding of generated samples, it cannot be parallelized over the forecast horizon — negating a key computational advantage. The text implies sequential sampling 'similar to seq-to-seq models', but this risks misrepresenting the method. A correct interpretation is that the decoder generates all conditioning vectors **ĥ**_{t_0:T} in one forward pass using causal masking and teacher-forced inputs during training, but the inference description fails to clarify whether generated **x_t** are re-fed. This ambiguity jeopardizes reproducibility and weakens the claim of efficient parallel inference.

comments.title: Ambiguous and potentially misleading procedure for cross-covariance matrix visualization
comments.quote: Figure 5: Visual analysis of the dependency structure extrapolation of the model. _Left_ : Cross-covariance matrix computed from the test split of Traffic benchmark. _Middle_ : Cross-covariance matrix computed from the mean of 100 sample trajectories drawn from the Transformer-MAF model’s extrapolation into the future (test split).
comments.explanation: The description states that the model's cross-covariance is computed from the 'mean of 100 sample trajectories', which suggests averaging trajectories first and then computing covariance. This would only reflect the second-order structure of the mean prediction, discarding sample variability and failing to assess the model's ability to capture uncertain dependencies. The correct approach would be to compute the cross-covariance per sample and then average the matrices. Although the authors may have performed this correctly, the wording ambiguously implies a methodologically flawed procedure that undermines the validity of Figure 5’s conclusions. Given that dependency modeling is central to the paper’s claims, this lack of clarity constitutes a serious exposition issue.

comments.title: Inconsistent time indexing in inference and evaluation setup
comments.quote: The attention model similarly uses a warm-up time series **x** 1: _t_ 1 _−_ 1 and covariates...
comments.explanation: The paper uses _t_0 to denote the start of the prediction window in formal expressions, but switches to _t_1 in the inference description without definition or justification. This inconsistency creates confusion about whether prediction starts at a fixed index or varies across experiments. Combined with the statement **ĥ**_1 = \vec{0}, it implies a global start at t=1, which is incompatible with rolling-window evaluations that begin after observing historical data. While context may resolve the intent — using available history when present, zero initialization otherwise — the notational shift obscures the cold-start mechanism and risks misinterpretation of the inference protocol.

comments.title: Underexplained omission of temperature scaling in sampling
comments.quote: Note that we do _not_ sample from a reduced-temperature model, e.g. by scaling the variance of the isotropic Gaussian, unlike what is done in likelihood-based generative models (Parmar et al., 2018) to obtain higher quality samples.
comments.explanation: The paper correctly avoids temperature scaling (which distorts uncertainty), but only frames it as a difference from prior work rather than a principled necessity for calibrated probabilistic forecasting. A brief justification — such as preserving predictive uncertainty and maintaining proper calibration — would strengthen the argument. Without it, readers may perceive this as an arbitrary implementation choice rather than a critical design decision, potentially leading to misguided attempts to 'sharpen' samples. The lack of explicit rationale underspecifies a key aspect of valid probabilistic inference.

comments.title: Overstated claim of MAF superiority due to 'density modeling performance'
comments.quote: Table 1 shows that swapping the Real NVP flow with a MAF improved the performance, which is a consequence of Real NVP lacking in density modeling performance compared to MAF.
comments.explanation: The authors attribute MAF's better performance solely to superior 'density modeling performance', implying an intrinsic advantage. However, MAF and Real NVP have architectural trade-offs: MAF is more expressive per transformation but slower, while Real NVP is more parallelizable. The performance gap in this context may stem from better conditioning integration, training dynamics, or task-specific factors — not a general deficiency of Real NVP. Asserting a global superiority without ablation studies or discussion of trade-offs overgeneralizes and misrepresents a context-dependent result as a fundamental property, which could mislead future design choices.

comments.title: Incomplete justification for dequantization in discrete data handling
comments.quote: For discrete data the distribution has differential entropy of negative infinity, which leads to arbitrary high likelihood when training normalizing flow models, even on test data. To avoid this one can _dequantize_ the data, often by adding Uniform[0 _,_ 1) noise to integer-valued data.
comments.explanation: The explanation for dequantization is correct in essence — discrete data assigned to continuous models can have infinite likelihood under differential entropy — but the paper states this as a definitive fact without qualifying that the issue arises specifically when treating discrete data as continuous without a proper density correction. The solution (adding uniform noise) is standard, but the reasoning skips over the fact that the resulting likelihood is a lower bound on the discrete likelihood, which is critical for valid training. The paper mentions this point later in the same paragraph via reference to Theis et al. (2016), so the justification is not fully missing. However, the causal phrasing — 'leads to arbitrary high likelihood' — could suggest that normalizing flows fundamentally fail on discrete data, which is not true if properly dequantized. The issue is minor because the correct method and reference are provided, but the initial statement lacks nuance.

comments.explanation: The text describes conditioning the flow on **h** by concatenating it to **x**_{1:d} inside the functions s() and t(). From context, the authors clearly mean the first d dimensions of the current input **x**, consistent with the coupling layer definition in the preamble. The intent—conditioning s and t on both the split input and an external vector **h**—is standard and correctly applied in normalizing flows. The ambiguity arises only from the notation: **x**[1:]_[d] looks like a malformed attempt to write **x**_{1:d}. Given that the surrounding text and definitions use **x**_{1:d} consistently, and that s and t are defined as functions from ℝ^d → ℝ^{D−d}, this is likely an OCR artifact. Still, without assuming correction, a naive reader might interpret **x**[1:] as a sequence from time 1 onward, creating confusion about whether conditioning occurs across time. This does not affect the method's validity but could mislead in reproduction.

comments.title: Incorrect normalization factor in scaled dot-product attention
comments.quote: we normalize by _dK_ the dimension of the **W** _h[K]_ matrices
comments.explanation: In the description of scaled dot-product attention, the text says attention is normalized by _dK_, the dimension of the key matrices. This aligns with the standard formulation, where the dot products are scaled by 1/√(dK) to stabilize gradients. However, the equation is missing from the passage—only the prose is present. The statement 'we normalize by _dK_' is ambiguous: it could be misread as dividing by dK itself rather than √(dK), which would be incorrect. In standard Transformer literature (Vaswani et al., 2017), the scaling is explicitly 1/√(d_k). While experts would interpret 'normalize by dK' as shorthand for the correct scaling, a careless reader might implement it as 1/dK. The context does not resolve this fully—there is no equation to confirm the correct form. Given the widespread understanding of this mechanism, and that the authors cite Vaswani et al., this is likely not an error in intent. But the lack of precise formulation (e.g., writing 'scale by 1/√(d_k)') introduces a small risk of misimplementation.

comments.title: Misstatement of dequantization log-likelihood bound for discrete data
comments.quote: The log-likelihood of the resulting continuous model is then lower-bounded by the log-likelihood of the discrete one as shown in Theis et al. (2016)
comments.explanation: The passage claims that the log-likelihood of the dequantized continuous model is *lower-bounded* by that of the discrete model. This contradicts the known result from Theis et al. (2016), which establishes that the continuous log-likelihood *upper-bounds* the discrete one. Specifically, when you dequantize discrete data X by adding uniform noise U ~ Uniform[0,1), forming a continuous variable Y = X + U, then log p_Y(y) ≥ log p_X(x) + const, meaning the continuous density's log-likelihood is an upper bound (up to a constant) on the discrete one. This is critical because maximizing the continuous likelihood optimizes a variational upper bound on the discrete entropy. The paper reverses the direction: claiming the continuous model is *lower*-bounded by the discrete would imply weaker statistical guarantees. This error appears to be a verbal slip—contextually, the authors are defending dequantization as a valid training approach, which only works if the continuous objective bounds the discrete one above. This is a clear mismatch with standard theory, and could mislead a reader about the theoretical justification for dequantization.

comments.title: Ambiguity in parameter sharing via RNN/Attention across conditioners
comments.quote: we can share parameters across the different conditioners **h** _t_ by using RNNs or Attention in an autoregressive fashion
comments.explanation: The authors state that RNNs or Attention allow parameter sharing across conditioners **h_t**, which is correct in spirit—RNNs share weights across time, and Transformers reuse attention mechanisms. However, the phrasing is vague about what exactly is shared and how. In the RNN case, it's clear: a single RNN produces h_t from history, and those h_t condition separate flow layers at each t. But the paper does not clarify whether the flow parameters themselves are shared across time steps or whether a new flow is instantiated per t. Given that the proposed model is autoregressive and trained end-to-end, it's likely that the flow parameters are shared across time, with only the conditioner h_t varying. This enables parameter efficiency and generalization. But without explicit statement, a reader might assume separate flows per time step, which would be infeasible for long horizons. The later section on inference clarifies that h_t is updated sequentially, suggesting shared parameters. Still, the passage underdescribes a key architectural choice: whether the normalizing flow itself has time-dependent parameters or not. This impacts reproducibility.

comments.title: Incorrect conditioning state in RNN update description
comments.quote: its hidden state **h** _t_ is updated given the previous time step observation **x** _t−_ 1 and the current time step’s covariates **c** _t_
comments.explanation: At first glance, this seems consistent with standard RNN formulations. However, the model is described as being autoregressive in **x_t**, meaning that the current observation **x_t** should condition the distribution at time t, and the RNN state **h_t** should summarize information up to time t−1 to condition **x_t**. The concern is that updating **h_t** based on **x_{t−1}** and **c_t** implies **h_t** depends on **c_t**, which is contemporaneous with **x_t**. But if **c_t** includes future-known features (e.g., current hour), it may be valid. Still, the more serious issue is that the RNN update is described as using **x_{t−1}** and **c_t**, yet in Figure 1 the input to the RNN is labeled as **x_{t−1}** and **c_t**, and the output is **h_t**, which is then used to condition **x_t**—this is acceptable. The real problem arises in Section 4.3, where inference conditions **x_{t1}** on **ĥ_{t1}**, which is computed from **x_{1:t1−1}**, suggesting **h_t** should depend on **x_{t−1}** and past covariates. But here, **c_t** (current covariates) are used to compute **h_t**, which is then used to model **x_t**. While this is common practice (e.g., DeepAR uses **c_t** to compute distribution parameters for **x_t**), the issue is that **c_t** is treated both as input to RNN state update and as part of the conditioning for **x_t**, which can be reasonable. However, the sentence gives the impression that **h_t** is computed from **x_{t−1}** and **c_t**, but it omits whether **h_{t−1}** is also used, which is essential for autoregressive state updates. The phrase 'updated given' is underspecified: in RNNs, **h_t = f(h_{t−1}, x_{t−1}, c_t)**, not just from **x_{t−1}** and **c_t**. Without mentioning **h_{t−1}**, the update is misleading and underspecified. This omission could mislead a reader into thinking the RNN is stateless or non-recurrent. The model schematic in Figure 1 shows **h_{t−1} → h_t**, so the full dependency is implied visually but not stated. Since the text claims 'this model is autoregressive since it consumes [...] the recurrent state **h_{t−1}**', it does acknowledge the recurrence, but the prior sentence fails to include **h_{t−1}** in the update description.

comments.title: Ambiguous time indexing in covariate and data slicing notation
comments.explanation: This appears in the TRAINING section as part of the SGD objective description. The notation is highly ambiguous: 'x 1: _t_ 0−1−1' likely means **x_{1:t_0−2}**, since _t_0−1−1 = t_0−2. Similarly, 'x _t_ 0:: _T_ −1' suggests **x_{t_0:T−1}**, which is plausible as lagged inputs for autoregressive prediction. But 'c _t_ 0+1:+1: _T_' is poorly formatted—'+1:' is likely an OCR artifact for '_{t_0+1:T}_'. However, the structure suggests the model uses **c_{2:t_0}** and **c_{t_0+1:T}**, which might be correct. The concern is that **c_t** is used at time t, so **c_{t_0:T}** would be appropriate for modeling **x_{t_0:T}**. But why start **c** at index 2? And why use **c_{t_0+1:T}** for the target period? This might reflect a shift: perhaps the model predicts **x_t** using **c_t**, but during training, the covariates for the prediction window are known only from **t_0+1** onward. Yet this still doesn’t justify starting **c** at 2. The more plausible explanation is OCR corruption: 'x 1: _t_ 0−1−1' probably should be **x_{1:t_0−1}**, and '−1−1' a misread of '−1'. Similarly, '::' could be '::' or ':-1'. Given that in Section 4, the context window is [1, t_0), so **x_{1:t_0−1}** is context, and prediction is [t_0, T], suggesting **x_{t_0:T}** is predicted. The training objective likely uses **x_{1:t_0−1}** as input and **x_{t_0:T}** as output, with associated **c_t**. The covariates **c_{2:t_0}** and **c_{t_0+1:T}** may be misaligned. However, since **c_t** could include lagged values or be defined at t=2 due to differencing, it’s hard to rule out. Given OCR likelihood and surrounding context, this is probably a notation error introduced in extraction, not an author error. We should not flag it as a real issue.

comments.title: Missing Jacobian dependence on conditioning in flow model
comments.quote: This does not change the log-determinant of the coupling layers given by (2).
comments.explanation: This sentence appears in Section 2.1 and reappears in the background reasoning: when conditioning a Real NVP via concatenation of **h** to the inputs of s() and t(), the log-determinant remains ∑ s(·), same as unconditional. The authors are correct: since **h** is treated as fixed conditioning information, it does not affect the derivative of the transformation with respect to **x**, so the Jacobian determinant depends only on s(), not on **h**. This is standard in conditional normalizing flows (e.g., cINN, conditional Real NVP). The log-det remains ∑ s(concat(x_{1:d}, h)), which is a function of **x** and **h**, but its form is unchanged—only its input is expanded. The statement is accurate: the *form* of the log-det is the same, just evaluated on augmented input. Thus, no error here. Context resolves the concern.

comments.title: Ambiguity in autoregressive factorization scope
comments.quote: As the model is autoregressive it can be written as a product of factors
comments.explanation: The sentence is followed by an equation that is cut off, but the intent is that the joint distribution p(**x_{t_0:T}** | history) is factorized as ∏_{t=t_0}^T p(**x_t** | past), which is standard. However, within each time step **x_t**, if the normalizing flow is autoregressive (e.g., MAF), it further factorizes p(**x_t** | **h_t**) = ∏_{i=1}^D p(x_t^{[i]} | x_t^{[1:i−1]}, **h_t**). The issue is that the term 'autoregressive' is used at two levels: (1) over time (modeling **x_t** conditioned on past **x**), and (2) within **x_t** if using MAF. The authors do not clarify whether the factorization mentioned refers to temporal autoregression or component-wise. Given that the flow is a joint distribution model over **x_t**, the autoregressive property in the text likely refers to the temporal dependence. But without seeing the full equation, it’s ambiguous. However, given that the model uses a flow conditioned on **h_t**, and **h_t** captures temporal dynamics, the factorization is over time, not components. The statement is acceptable given context, though could be clearer.

comments.title: Understated complexity of flow stacking with RNN
comments.quote: we stack _K_ layers of a conditional flow module (Real NVP or MAF) and together with the RNN, we arrive at our model
comments.explanation: The authors correctly describe stacking K flow layers conditioned on RNN state **h_t**. However, the computational complexity is not discussed in this context. While the Transformer section notes O(T^2 D) vs O(T D^2), the RNN + flow model's complexity is not analyzed. If the RNN is O(T D^2) and each flow layer is O(D) per time step (due to triangular or block-triangular Jacobian), the full model remains O(T D^2) from the RNN. But since the flow has K layers, and each coupling layer applies s() and t() networks that may be MLPs of width W, the per-step cost is O(K W D), which could dominate if K or W is large. The claim that the model is 'computationally tractable' is supported by results, but this specific combination's complexity is underspecified. However, this is a detail likely covered in implementation or appendix. Not a major flaw.

comments.title: Unclear masking in Transformer decoder during training
comments.quote: via a causally masked decoder stack
comments.explanation: The authors state that a causal mask is used to prevent future leakage, which is correct. In Figure 2 and text, they emphasize parallel training and cold-start capability. However, in a standard autoregressive Transformer (like GPT), the decoder self-attention is causally masked so that position t only attends to 1..t−1. But here, the decoder is used to generate conditioning for **x_{t_0:T}**, and if it autoregressively generates one step at a time, how is batching done? The text says 'all **x**_{1:T} time points are trained in parallel', which suggests full target sequence is available during training—i.e., teacher forcing. This is standard, but the explanation is underspecified: it does not explicitly state that during training, the true **x_{t_0:T}** are used as input to the decoder (with causal masking), while at inference, decoding is iterative. This is implied by 'cold-start' and 'iteratively sample', but not clearly stated. For reproducibility, this should be clarified. The method description is underspecified in this regard, though experienced readers would assume teacher forcing.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Transformer Inference Procedure
comments.quote: The attention model similarly uses a warm-up time series **x** 1: _t_ 1 _−_ 1 and covariates and passes them through the encoder and then uses the decoder to output the conditioning for sampling from the flow. This sample is then used again in the decoder to iteratively sample the next conditioning state, similar to the inference procedure in seq-to-seq models.
comments.explanation: At first glance, this description of the Transformer-based inference appears to suggest an autoregressive decoding process where each sampled **x**_t is fed back into the decoder to generate the next conditioning **ĥ**_t. This would align with standard seq-to-seq sampling (e.g., in language models). However, this contradicts the earlier claim in the PAPER SUMMARY (Section 4.1) that the Transformer model enables *parallel* computation over time steps during training *and* inference. If the model truly required iterative feeding of sampled outputs into the decoder — as implied here — then inference could not be parallelized over the forecast horizon. This creates confusion: is inference autoregressive (sequential) or parallel? The key distinction lies in whether the Transformer decoder conditions on previously generated samples. Given that the paper emphasizes parallel training and computational complexity advantages, it is more likely that the model uses the full context **x**_{1:t_0−1} in the encoder and generates all conditioning vectors **ĥ**_{t_0:T} in one forward pass via causal masking, without re-feeding sampled **x**_t. The quoted text misleadingly suggests a sequential autoregressive sampling loop akin to RNNs. While the phrase 'similar to seq-to-seq models' might be intended as an analogy for conditioning, it risks implying a method that undermines the model’s claimed efficiency advantage. The procedure is underspecified: it does not clarify whether the decoder inputs are only the warm-up data and covariates (enabling parallelism) or whether generated samples are fed back (requiring autoregression).

comments.title: Missing Justification for CRPSsum Definition
comments.quote: CRPSsum is obtained by first summing across the _F_ ˆsum( _t_ ) for each time point). _D_ time-series—both for the ground-truth data, and sampled data (yieldingThe results are then averaged over the prediction horizon, i.e. formally
comments.explanation: This sentence is clearly corrupted by OCR or formatting issues — 'yieldingThe results' suggests a line break or hyphenation error — but beyond that, the definition of CRPSsum is underspecified. The text claims CRPS is computed on the sum of all time series, which is valid, but the formal procedure is ambiguous. Specifically: do we compute the CRPS of the summed time series (i.e., compare sum_{i=1}^D x_i(t) to sum_{i=1}^D \hat{x}_i(t)), or do we sum the individual CRPS values? The latter would not be meaningful for evaluating joint behavior. The former — evaluating distributional accuracy on aggregate — is common in forecasting (e.g., total demand). However, the text says 'first summing across the ... time-series—both for the ground-truth data, and sampled data', which supports the correct interpretation. Yet the incomplete equation and lack of formal expression (e.g., CRPS(F_{\text{sum}}, x_{\text{sum}})) leave room for misinterpretation. While domain experts would likely infer the correct meaning, a careful reader might wonder whether CRPSsum aggregates scores or aggregates trajectories. Given that the evaluation metric underpins the paper’s key claim of 'state-of-the-art results', precise definition is essential. The explanation would be greatly improved by a clear formula.

comments.title: Inconsistent Use of t_0 and t_1 in Inference Description
comments.quote: The attention model similarly uses a warm-up time series **x** 1: _t_ 1 _−_ 1 and covariates...
comments.explanation: The paper uses _t_0 to denote the start of the prediction window in the formal setup (e.g., in the likelihood expression: \prod_{t=t_0}^T). However, in the inference section (4.3), it shifts to using _t_1 without defining it. While it is reasonable to assume _t_1 = _t_0 (the first prediction step), the switch in indexing is inconsistent and could confuse readers. Why not use _t_0 throughout? The definition of cold-start inference as setting **ĥ**_1 = \vec{0} contrasts with **ĥ**_{t_1} — unless _t_1 = 1, which is not generally the case in rolling-window evaluation. This suggests a possible index misalignment: setting **ĥ**_1 = \vec{0} only makes sense if inference starts at t=1. But in rolling evaluation, inference often starts after a long history. The text likely means: if no history is available, set the initial state to zero; otherwise, warm up using available history **x**_{1:pred_start−1}. But the use of _t_1 muddles this, and **ĥ**_1 = \vec{0} seems to imply a global start at t=1, not cold-start at an arbitrary forecast origin. This is a notation inconsistency that, while resolvable from context, risks misinterpretation of the cold-start mechanism.

comments.title: Incomplete Justification for Ignoring Temperature Scaling
comments.quote: Note that we do _not_ sample from a reduced-temperature model, e.g. by scaling the variance of the isotropic Gaussian, unlike what is done in likelihood-based generative models (Parmar et al., 2018) to obtain higher quality samples.
comments.explanation: The statement acknowledges a known technique (temperature scaling) to sharpen samples in generative models but dismisses it without justification. In probabilistic forecasting, preserving the true predictive uncertainty is essential — unlike in image generation, where sharper (but possibly less diverse) samples may be preferred. Thus, avoiding temperature scaling is *correct* here, as it would distort the predicted uncertainty and harm proper calibration. However, the paper frames the choice only as a difference from prior work, not as a *requirement* for valid probabilistic forecasting. A brief justification — e.g., 'to maintain calibrated uncertainty estimates' — would strengthen the argument. As written, it reads like an implementation detail rather than a principled decision. While experts may infer the reasoning, the claim is underspecified and could mislead readers into thinking temperature scaling is a viable improvement, when in fact it would compromise the model's probabilistic integrity.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of cross-covariance matrix in Figure 5
comments.quote: Figure 5: Visual analysis of the dependency structure extrapolation of the model. _Left_ : Cross-covariance matrix computed from the test split of Traffic benchmark. _Middle_ : Cross-covariance matrix computed from the mean of 100 sample trajectories drawn from the Transformer-MAF model’s extrapolation into the future (test split).
comments.explanation: At first glance, the term 'cross-covariance matrix' appears to refer to the covariance between different time series (i.e., across the D dimensions), which aligns with the paper’s focus on modeling spatial dependencies. However, the phrase 'computed from the mean of 100 sample trajectories' raises concern: if the cross-covariance is computed from the averaged trajectories, it would reflect only the covariance of the *mean predictions*, not the full model’s ability to capture second-order structure in the predictive distribution. That is, taking the mean across 100 samples before computing covariance discards the sample-to-sample variability and may not reflect the true model-estimated cross-covariance. The authors likely intend to compute the cross-covariance *per sample trajectory* and then average those matrices—but the current wording suggests averaging the trajectories first. Given the paper’s emphasis on capturing spatial dependencies (e.g., Cov(S₁, S₂) > 0) and the claim that the model reflects 'most of the covariance structure', this distinction matters. While the context mentions 100 samples are used (consistent with CRPS estimation), this specific procedure for Figure 5 is underspecified. The appendix may clarify, but within the provided context, the description is ambiguous enough that a reader could misinterpret how the model’s uncertainty and dependency structure are evaluated.

comments.title: Potential misuse of 'covariance' vs. 'empirical cross-covariance' in visual analysis
comments.quote: To showcase how well our model captures dependencies in extrapolating the time series into the future versus real data, we plot in Figure 5 the cross-covariance matrix of observations (plotted left) as well as the mean of 100 sample trajectories (middle plot) drawn from Transformer-MAF model for the test split of Traffic data set.
comments.explanation: The left panel is described as the 'cross-covariance matrix computed from the test split', which presumably means the empirical covariance across the D time series at the same time points, i.e., Cov(S_i, S_j) estimated from test data. This is standard. However, the model’s output is described as the 'mean of 100 sample trajectories'. If this mean trajectory is treated as a deterministic time series and then a single cross-covariance matrix is computed from it, this would be misleading—because it ignores the model’s probabilistic nature. A correct approach would be to compute the expected cross-covariance under the predictive distribution, either by averaging the covariance matrices computed from each of the 100 sampled trajectories, or by using second-order moments of the joint predictive distribution. The current wording risks implying that the authors are evaluating the covariance structure of the forecast *mean only*, which does not assess whether the model captures *uncertain dependencies*—a key goal of probabilistic modeling. While the authors may have done the correct computation, the description fails to clarify how the ensemble of trajectories is aggregated for this plot. This is a moderate issue because accurate visual validation of dependency structure is central to the paper’s claim of capturing multivariate dynamics.

comments.title: Missing temporal offset in cross-covariance interpretation for physical flow model
comments.quote: To showcase how well our model captures dependencies in extrapolating the time series into the future versus real data, we plot in Figure 5 the cross-covariance matrix of observations [...] As can be seen, most of the covariance structure especially in the top-left region of highly correlated sensors is very well reflected in the samples drawn from the model.
comments.explanation: The paper’s simulation section (PAPER SUMMARY) states that 'effects propagate from S₀(t) to S₁(t+1), S₂(t+1) to S₃(t+2)', indicating a *temporal lag* in the cross-covariance structure—e.g., S₀ and S₁ should be correlated but with a lag. However, Figure 5 shows a *contemporaneous* cross-covariance matrix (Cov(S_i, S_j) at the same time t), which may not reflect this lagged dependency. The paper claims the model learns 'temporal cross-covariance structure consistent with physical flow dynamics', but if Figure 5 only evaluates zero-lag covariance, it may miss the lagged interactions that are physically meaningful. It's possible the model induces strong contemporaneous correlation via the shared source S₀, and this is what dominates the matrix. But without clarification, a reader might conclude the model captures lagged dynamics when only zero-lag correlations are being verified. This doesn’t invalidate the claim entirely, but it weakens the support from Figure 5. The context does confirm that physical lags exist, so the evaluation should ideally include lagged cross-covariances or impulse response plots. The current presentation oversimplifies the dependency structure.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of 'CRPSsum' in prose vs. formal definition
comments.quote: In Table 1 we observe that MAF with either RNN or self-attention mechanism for temporal conditioning achieves the state-of-the-art (to the best of our knowledge) CRPSsum on all benchmarks.
comments.explanation: The paper defines CRPSsum as the average CRPS of the *summed* time series: 'CRPSsum = 1/T ∑_{t=t₀}^T CRPS(Ḟ_sum(t), x_sum(t))', where both the predictive distribution and the ground truth are summed across D series. However, in the text, CRPSsum is described as being evaluated 'on all benchmarks', without specifying whether all benchmarks support a meaningful sum. For example, count data from Taxi or Wikipedia may represent disjoint geographical or topical units—summing them may lack semantic meaning. The model summary confirms dequantization is used for these datasets, but doesn’t clarify if summing is appropriate. The assumption that CRPSsum is universally meaningful across datasets is questionable. While the authors correctly define CRPSsum formally and it is valid for the Traffic dataset (where total flow may be of interest), its application to other datasets without justification risks overclaiming. The prose treats CRPSsum as a universal metric, but its interpretability depends on the application. This is a minor issue because the metric is still mathematically well-defined, but the claim of 'state-of-the-art on all benchmarks' via CRPSsum could be misleading without domain-specific validation.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about MAF outperforming Real NVP due to 'density modeling performance'
comments.quote: Table 1 shows that swapping the Real NVP flow with a MAF improved the performance, which is a consequence of Real NVP lacking in density modeling performance compared to MAF.
comments.explanation: The authors assert that MAF outperforms Real NVP due to superior 'density modeling performance', implying an inherent capability difference. However, MAF and Real NVP are architecturally different: MAF is autoregressive in transformation (allowing richer conditional modeling), while Real NVP uses coupling layers with fixed dependency order. In high-dimensional time series, MAF may better capture dependencies *in this setup*, but declaring it globally superior in 'density modeling performance' is an overgeneralization. The performance gap could stem from conditioning effectiveness, training dynamics, or the specific way temporal context is integrated, rather than intrinsic density estimation capacity. Moreover, Real NVP with more layers or better conditioning might close the gap. The authors are correct that MAF performs better here, but attributing it solely to density modeling capability without ablation or discussion of architectural differences is questionable. Standard literature (e.g., Papamakarios et al.) notes trade-offs between speed and flexibility, but does not claim MAF is universally superior.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The introduction and background sections of this paper demonstrate a high level of academic rigor, effectively positioning the proposed method within the context of contemporary deep learning research. The authors clearly articulate the tension between the necessity of modeling complex inter-series dependencies and the computational burden of high-dimensional distributions, providing a strong motivation for the use of conditioned normalizing flows. The text is technically sound, exhibiting clear logical flow and precise mathematical notation, which establishes credibility. The most significant challenge for the remainder of the paper will be to maintain this clarity when reconciling the inherently sequential nature of autoregressive modeling with the parallelizability of the proposed Transformer-based architecture, as well as providing convincing evidence that the computational complexity of normalizing flows remains manageable at the scale of "several thousand interacting time-series." Overall, the work shows excellent promise, provided the experimental section sufficiently addresses the trade-offs between model expressivity and inference latency.

comments.title: Inaccurate dependence condition in MAF description
comments.quote: any output y[i] cannot depend on input with dimension indices ≥ i, i.e. x[≥][i]
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly suggests that autoregressive mappings cannot depend on their own current dimension index (i). By definition, y[i] must be a function of x[1:i]; the constraint only precludes dependence on future indices (> i). The current phrasing misrepresents the requirements for model expressivity.

comments.title: Ambiguous computational complexity comparison between Transformer and RNN
comments.quote: The Transformer has computational complexity O(T^2D) compared to a RNN which is O(TD^2)
comments.explanation: The complexity comparison is underspecified. The O(TD^2) figure for RNNs is highly architecture-dependent (e.g., assuming a full DxD covariance matrix). Since the proposed model uses a Normalizing Flow to likely reduce this overhead, the authors must clarify the complexity of their model specifically, or acknowledge how the flow alters the O(TD^2) baseline. Labeling it broadly as 'RNN complexity' conflates the bottleneck of the baseline versus the proposed method.

comments.title: Malformed and inconsistent indexing notation
comments.explanation: The indexing notation across the text and Figure 2 is internally inconsistent and contains apparent OCR artifacts (double subtractions, double colons, and inconsistent range starts). Specifically, index ranges like 'x_{1:t_0-1-1}' and the unexplained start of covariates at index 2 (c_{2:t_0}) versus the context window [1, t_0) create significant ambiguity for reproducibility and implementation.

progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.label: Progressive Original (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive_original__gemini-3.1-flash-l.overall_feedback: The introduction and background sections of this paper demonstrate a high level of academic rigor, effectively positioning the proposed method within the context of contemporary deep learning research. The authors clearly articulate the tension between the necessity of modeling complex inter-series dependencies and the computational burden of high-dimensional distributions, providing a strong motivation for the use of conditioned normalizing flows. The text is technically sound, exhibiting clear logical flow and precise mathematical notation, which establishes credibility. The most significant challenge for the remainder of the paper will be to maintain this clarity when reconciling the inherently sequential nature of autoregressive modeling with the parallelizability of the proposed Transformer-based architecture, as well as providing convincing evidence that the computational complexity of normalizing flows remains manageable at the scale of "several thousand interacting time-series." Overall, the work shows excellent promise, provided the experimental section sufficiently addresses the trade-offs between model expressivity and inference latency.

comments.title: Inaccurate dependence condition in MAF description
comments.quote: any output y[i] cannot depend on input with dimension indices ≥ i, i.e. x[≥][i]
comments.explanation: In an autoregressive flow (like MAF), the output dimension y[i] must not depend on input dimension indices > i (the future indices). However, it is fundamentally required for the model's expressivity that y[i] *does* depend on the input indices <= i. The text incorrectly states that it cannot depend on indices >= i. While context implies the standard autoregressive property, the inclusion of the 'equal to' case (i) and the specific notation x[>=][i] suggests a restriction that would prevent the variable from depending on its own current dimension, which is incorrect for autoregressive mapping.

comments.title: Implicitly defined or missing Jacobian determinant formula
comments.quote: where ∂f( x ) /∂ x is the Jacobian of f at x . Normalizing flows have the property that the inverse x = f[-][1] ( z ) is easy to evaluate and computing the Jacobian determinant takes O ( D ) time.
comments.explanation: The text introduces the Jacobian in the context of the change of variables formula but skips the actual equation or the explicit expression of the log-determinant. While the paper later provides the log-determinant for Real NVP (eq 3), it makes a general claim about O(D) complexity for normalizing flows in Section 2.1 without clarifying that this is specific to certain architectures (like triangular Jacobians). This is a bit ambiguous as a general property, though likely intended to be context-specific to the methods discussed.

comments.title: Index indexing/notation mismatch
comments.quote: The Transformer has computational complexity O(T^2D) compared to a RNN which is O(TD^2)
comments.explanation: In standard RNN-based multivariate time series forecasting (like the Vec-LSTM or DeepAR-style models cited), the computational cost per time step for hidden state updates is typically proportional to the size of the weight matrices linking the latent state of dimension H to the output of dimension D. If H is proportional to D (as specified by the authors: 'dimension of the hidden states are proportional to the number of simultaneous time-series'), the cost of an RNN cell per step is O(D^2). Over T steps, this is O(TD^2). However, for the Transformer, 'O(T^2D)' correctly reflects the self-attention complexity per layer. The concern is that the comparison implies the Transformer is 'more efficient' for large D, but the scaling with D is linear for the Transformer (O(T^2D)) versus quadratic for the RNN (O(TD^2)). While technically correct in terms of asymptotic notation in D, it conflates the memory-bound vs compute-bound nature of these models. More importantly, the 'O(TD^2)' complexity for RNNs assumes a dense dependency structure; if the RNN is factorized (as in DeepAR), it is O(TD). The authors' claim of efficiency depends entirely on the assumption that D is very large, which is acceptable, but the labeling of the complexity terms could mislead a reader about the specific bottleneck (memory vs. depth).

comments.title: Ambiguous index notation in sequence ranges
comments.quote: via SGD using Adam (Kingma & Ba, 2015) with x_1:t_0−1−1 ... with respect to the parameters θ
comments.explanation: The OCR has produced 'x_1:t_0−1−1', which likely represents a range x_{1:t_0-1}. The inclusion of an extra '-1' is almost certainly an OCR artifact or a typo. Given the context of training over a history context window [1, t_0), the notation 'x_{1:t_0-1}' is consistent with the preceding text. I am flagging this because in a formal academic paper, the index notation for the training objective objective is critical for implementation reproduction, and a trailing '-1' could be interpreted as an off-by-one error or an index shift that would break the causal masking of the Transformer.

comments.title: Potentially circular or undefined reference to observation indices in Transformer notation
comments.explanation: In Figure 2's caption and the associated text, the indexing notation for the time series and covariates is confusing. The notation 'x_{1: t_ 0−1−1}' appears to contain a double subtraction or a typo in index range definition. Looking back at the text, the split point is 't_0'. The notation 'x_{t_ 0:: T −1}' also appears non-standard with the double colon and an inconsistent trailing '-1'. While I understand the intent is to define the context (1 to t_0-1) and target (t_0 to T) windows, the literal transcription is mathematically ambiguous and syntactically malformed.

comments.title: Complexity class notation inconsistency
comments.quote: The Transformer has computational complexity O(T^2D) compared to a RNN which is O(TD^2)
comments.explanation: The authors claim the Transformer has complexity O(T^2D) and the RNN has O(TD^2). While standard self-attention is indeed O(T^2D), the claim that an RNN (modeling D interacting time series) is O(TD^2) is highly dependent on the architecture of the 'emission' model. If the RNN hidden state is mapped to the parameters of a multivariate Gaussian (requiring a covariance matrix of size DxD), then O(D^2) is correct. However, if the normalizing flow approach (which is the core contribution) replaces the D^2 parameterization with a flow operation (which usually operates in O(D) or O(KD) where K is number of layers), the overall complexity argument may be misrepresenting the efficiency gain provided by the flow specifically versus the autoregressive backbone. It is not entirely clear if the 'O(TD^2)' refers to the baseline 'Vec-LSTM' or their new model.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing of covariates in Figure 2 schematic
comments.explanation: In the Figure 2 caption, the covariate range 'c_{2: t_ 0}' is used. Given the context (Section 4) states that the context window is [1, t_0), it is unclear why the covariates start at index 2. This creates ambiguity for a reader attempting to implement the training loop. Is this a typo for 'c_{1:t_0}' or is there a specific alignment reason (e.g., the first covariate being ignored or shifted) that isn't explained in the text?
